text: The surgeon uses a ligature device to dissect the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus.
The surgeon uses a Ligature Atlas device, which measures 37 millimeters, to perform dissection.
The surgeon utilizes a supra umbilical port instead of the umbilical port for laparoscopic access during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the fallopian tube and major salpings using a ligature device, following the dissection of the round ligament approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus.
The surgeon identifies the ovarian ligament and prepares to dissect it as the third structure in sequence.
The surgeon navigates the telescope over the large uterus to visualize the right adnexal structures, with port positions arranged according to the baseball diamond concept.
The surgeon focuses on the left side, preparing to separate the round ligament using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses a ligature atlas to manipulate the fallopian tube, currently preserving it while considering future removal.
The surgeon identifies the ovarian ligament and positions the instrument near it to minimize bleeding, avoiding proximity to the uterus where bleeding risk is higher.
The light cable of the thirty-degree telescope is positioned at approximately eleven o'clock, providing an enhanced view over the bowel area.

The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to separate the uterovesical fold while pushing the uterus towards the five o'clock position. The left hand stretches the peritoneum anteriorly, revealing a clear areolar plane.
The surgeon demonstrates a clear plane of dissection at the uterovesical fold, with the uterus appearing retroverted due to uterine manipulation.
The surgeon uses the Rumi uterine manipulator, a full circle colpotomizer, to achieve precise control over the uterus during the procedure.

The surgeon separates the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, moving the ureter laterally. The vaginal fascia and surrounding tissue are further dissected, with any minor bleeding controlled using bipolar or harmonic devices. The uterus is repositioned from five o'clock to seven o'clock during the procedure.

The surgeon uses the left hand to stretch the peritoneum while the right hand operates the harmonic device to open the anterior leaf of the broad ligament on the right side, hooking it anterolaterally.

The surgeon uses the harmonic device, vibrating at high frequency, to create a precise plane of dissection, separating the bladder and opening the anterior leaf of the broad ligament without bleeding.
The surgeon examines the fissure tissue surrounding the uterine artery and identifies the cervicovaginal fissure, ensuring clear visualization of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon separates the remaining upper pedicles of the broad ligament, applying correct traction to facilitate the dissection.
The surgeon utilizes two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port to complete the procedure on this side, indicating the near completion of surgical actions in the current area.

The surgeon begins to take the uterine artery while noting the presence of remaining pedicles of the broad ligament that need separation. The anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament may also be opened during this process.

The surgeon uses the vibrating jaw of the harmonic device to hook and cut the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, performing minimal dissection. The uterine artery is taken with a ligature, allowing for easy coagulation.
The surgeon applies multiple coagulations to the uterine artery using a ligature device to minimize the risk of bleeding. After ensuring adequate coagulation, the uterine artery is cut.
The surgeon focuses on coagulating a vein located posteriorly, while noting the artery positioned anteriorly, requiring careful attention to avoid complications.
The surgeon exposes the lumen of the uterine structure, with the vein visible posteriorly. The mechanoid ligament is prepared for dissection.
The surgeon adjusts the ligature on the right side, ensuring the tip faces towards the cervix to prevent lateral movement during the procedure.

The surgeon positions the ligature medially to secure the uterine artery, ensuring that some ascending branches are also coagulated to prevent reverse bleeding. The proximity to the ureter is noted, requiring careful technique to avoid injury.
The surgeon performs a colpotomy, an incision made in the vaginal wall, while being cautious of the ureter to prevent injury, which is a known risk during total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH).
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to initiate a lateral colpotomy, cutting through the vaginal fascia at the specified level and also incising some of the mechanoid ligament.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a lateral colpotomy, starting from the lateral aspect without a suprapubic port. The tip of the harmonic scalpel is positioned near the vaginal tissue.
The harmonic scalpel smoothly cuts through the vaginal tissue, progressing to the right side while staying above the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon performs a right colpotomy, followed by a posterior colpotomy. The uterosacral ligament is visibly attached to the vaginal part.
The surgeon carefully cuts the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament, ensuring the incision is made smoothly at the level just touching the cervix to prevent wall prolapse.
The surgeon completes the dissection on the right side, having approached it from the left side, with the right side now fully dissected.
The surgeon addresses the remaining part of the left side of the vaginal vault and the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament, preparing to complete the dissection in this area.
The uterus is maneuvered into the uterine manipulator, completing the current stage of the procedure.
The surgeon performs a hysterectomy, bisecting the large uterus using two speculums. The uterus is then slowly extracted from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to gently rub and separate the remaining attached tissue from the uterus. The assistant manipulates the uterus slightly to aid in its separation, preparing for its removal.
The surgeon carefully bisects the large uterus to facilitate its removal, ensuring it is closer to being extracted from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Dandy jamming knot with continuous suturing, locking each stitch to secure the uterosacral corners. A sponge is visible inside the gloves, which are used to pack the vagina, preventing gas leakage.
The surgeon uses a glove as an occluder to prevent gas leakage, then proceeds to address the anterior leaf after completing the posterior section.

The surgeon includes the vaginal epithelium in the suturing process to prevent granulation tissue formation. Using a dandy jamming knot technique, the needle is passed through the loop, resulting in an automatic lock.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a starter knot for continuous suturing of the vaginal vault, carefully taking the posterior and anterior leaf one by one. The assistant holds the knot securely, ensuring it remains locked during the procedure.

The surgeon demonstrates closing the vaginal vault using continuous suturing with a locking technique, ensuring a secure closure. The tail of the suture is left long for final approximation at the corner.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the vaginal vault, taking sequential bites to interlock the suture and progressively close the tissue.

In the total laparoscopic hysterectomy, the surgeon positions the ports supra-umbilically and in the right and left iliac fossae, ensuring a 7.5 cm distance between them. The ports form an arc, allowing instruments to be at least 18 cm away from the target dissection site, which is the uterine artery.

During the closure of the vaginal vault, the surgeon uses instruments that function as a type three lever, resulting in magnified movements and slight tremors due to the increased distance of the instrument from the surgical site.
The surgeon approximates the corners of the surgical site using a Dundee jamming knot, with the tail of the suture kept long to facilitate closure.

The surgeon approximates the corners of the vault, providing support to the surrounding structures, including the uterosacral ligament. The procedure is terminated using a surgeon's knot, with the tail of the starting knot incorporated into the closure.

The surgeon performs a two-one-one double wrap suture followed by two alternating wraps to approximate the corners of the surgical site. Scissors are used to cut the suture, and suction is applied to remove minimal blood loss, approximately ten milliliters, ensuring a clean surgical field without bleeding.
